questions.question: Explain the difference between eof(), fail(), and bad() functions in C++ file handling.
keyPoints: eof() returns true when end-of-file is reached (eofbit is set)
fail() returns true for logical failures including EOF and type mismatch (failbit or eofbit)
bad() returns true only for fatal I/O errors like disk failure (badbit)
good() returns true only when all bits are clear (no errors, not at EOF)

questions.question: Write a C++ program to read a text file character by character until EOF and count the total characters.
keyPoints: Open file using ifstream
Use while(infile.get(ch)) or while(!infile.eof()) pattern
Increment counter inside loop
Close file and display count
Handle file open failure

questions.question: How is EOF detection different in binary files compared to text files in C++?
keyPoints: Binary files have no special EOF marker character
Text files may use Ctrl+D or Ctrl+Z as EOF in some systems
Binary EOF must be detected via stream state functions (eof(), fail(), read() return value)
In binary mode, any byte value (0-255) is valid data, cannot use character-based detection
Use read() with gcount() to determine actual bytes read

questions.question: Explain the purpose of clear() function and when should it be used.
keyPoints: clear() resets all error flags (eofbit, failbit, badbit) to goodbit
Used when you want to continue reading after hitting EOF
Stream remains in failed state after EOF until clear() is called
Example: after reading all data, call clear() to reset for potential rewinding

questions.question: Write a C++ program to read a file line by line using getline() until EOF.
keyPoints: Include <fstream> and <string>
Use ifstream infile("filename")
Declare string variable for line
Use while(std::getline(infile, line)) pattern
Process each line inside loop
getline() returns false at EOF automatically

questions.question: What is the significance of get() returning int instead of char?
keyPoints: Returns int to accommodate EOF value (-1) which is outside char range
char can hold 0-255 (or -128 to 127 signed)
EOF = -1 cannot be represented in unsigned char
Returning int allows distinguishing valid data (0-255) from EOF (-1)
Allows checking: if(ch == EOF) for end of file

questions.question: Explain why 'while(!infile.eof())' is considered a bad practice and suggest alternatives.
keyPoints: eof() only returns true AFTER attempting to read past EOF
Using while(!eof()) often processes last item twice
Better alternatives: while(infile.get(ch)), while(getline(infile, line)), while(infile >> var)
These check stream state directly, not the eof flag
The correct pattern: while(infile) { read and process }

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program that demonstrates multiple methods of EOF detection: using eof(), fail(), stream bool conversion, and peek(). Compare their behavior.
keyPoints: Create a sample text file with some content
Method 1: while(!infile.eof()) - explain it checks flag set after failed read
Method 2: while(infile.fail() == false) - more general failure check
Method 3: while(infile) - stream bool conversion
Method 4: while(infile.peek() != EOF) - look-ahead approach
Method 5: while(infile.get(ch)) - process each character
Include clear() between methods to reset stream
Show output and explain differences in when each loop terminates
Address edge cases: empty file, single line, exact buffer

questions.question: Explain the file stream state flags in C++ with a diagram showing their relationship. Write a program demonstrating how to handle different error conditions during file operations.
keyPoints: Explain goodbit (no errors), eofbit (end reached), failbit (logical error), badbit (fatal error)
When eofbit is set, fail() also returns true
bad() indicates critical failure - stream becomes unusable
good() returns true only when all flags are clear
Program should: open file, check if open, read data, detect EOF, handle errors
Use switch or if-else with eof(), fail(), bad(), good()
Show proper error messages for each condition
Demonstrate clear() to recover from non-fatal errors
Explain when to give up (badbit) vs retry (failbit)

questions.question: Write a C++ program to copy one binary file to another using proper EOF detection. Explain the challenges of EOF detection in binary files compared to text files.
keyPoints: Open source file in binary mode (ios::binary)
Open destination file in binary mode
Use read() and write() for binary data
Check EOF using: !infile.eof() or infile.fail() after read
Alternative: read into buffer and check gcount() for bytes read
Explain: binary files have no EOF marker character
Any byte value (0-255) is valid data in binary
Cannot use character-based detection in binary mode
Must rely on stream state or read return value
Show proper error handling for both files
Close files properly

questions.question: Design and explain a robust file reading system in C++ that handles: empty files, files with one line, files with multiple lines, files with whitespace, and corrupted/unreadable files. Use proper EOF detection throughout.
keyPoints: Initial check: if(!infile.is_open()) for file open failure
Empty file: should handle gracefully with eof() or !getline() returning false immediately
Single line: getline reads content, then returns false for EOF
Multiple lines: loop correctly until EOF
Whitespace handling: use ignore() or check for empty strings
Corrupted file: handle bad() - fatal I/O errors separately
Use multiple detection methods: getline(), eof(), fail(), good()
Reset stream with clear() if needed
Proper cleanup: close files, free resources
Show complete code with comments
Explain why certain detection methods work better for different scenarios
